senses.definition: to rest or sleep for a short period of time during the day

examples.x: I'm feeling a bit tired, so I'm going to take a nap for about 30 minutes before I continue working on my project.
During the weekend, it's nice to take a nap in the afternoon and catch up on some rest.
When I have a long flight, I like to take a nap on the plane to combat jet lag.
When the baby finally fell asleep, I took a nap to catch up on some much-needed rest.

senses.definition: to enroll and participate in an educational program or lesson

examples.x: I will take a course in Spanish next semester.
She took a class in art history to deepen her appreciation for various artistic movements.

senses.definition: to choose from existing options or alternatives

examples.x: I need to take a decision about which job offer to accept.
The board of directors will take a decision regarding the company's expansion plans.
I need to take a decision about which shirt to wear.
The team lead advised the group to take a decision regarding the project timeline from the suggested schedules.
The government needs to take a decision about how to deal with the refugee crisis.

senses.definition: to go on vacation or take time off from work or daily activities to relax and enjoy leisure activities

examples.x: She hasn't taken a holiday in years, so she really needs a break.
We will take a holiday during the summer to explore new destinations.
I took a holiday last month and traveled to Europe for a cultural experience

senses.definition: to learn or improve a skill through a class or guidance

examples.x: She takes a lesson in piano every Saturday to improve her musical skills.
They took a lesson in cooking to learn how to prepare authentic Italian dishes.

senses.definition: to use a device like a camera or cellphone to capture an image of something or someone

examples.x: I took a picture of the stunning sunset at the beach.
They will take a picture to remember this special moment.
We took a photo together to commemorate our friendship.

senses.definition: to temporarily stop physical or mental activity in order to relax and regain energy

examples.x: After the long hike, we decided to take a rest and enjoy the scenery.
I will take a rest before starting the next leg of the journey.
We took a rest in the park, sitting on a bench and enjoying the sunshine.

senses.definition: to respond to a series of questions designed to gather information, opinions, or feedback on a specific topic or subject

examples.x: Many customers were asked to take a survey about their shopping experience.
To gauge public opinion, the news organization invited viewers to take a survey about current events.

senses.definition: to stop working or doing an activity temporarily in order to rest, relax, etc.

examples.x: After hours of studying, she decided to take a break and go for a walk.
I need to take a break from studying; my brain is exhausted.
He took a break after finishing the first part of the project.

senses.definition: to go on a journey for enjoyment or exploration

examples.x: They took a trip to Europe and explored multiple countries.
In a few months, we will take a trip to visit our relatives.
After a busy year, he decided to take a trip to a tropical island.

word_list.pronunciation: tˈeɪk ˌɛsbˈiː slˈæʃ ˌɛstˌiːˈeɪtʃ tə kˈoːɹt

senses.definition: to start a legal process against someone or something to resolve a dispute

examples.x: They took their former business partner to court over a breach of contract.
She decides to take her landlord to court for refusing to make necessary repairs.
The company threatened to take the competitor to court for copyright infringement.
In cases of discrimination, individuals often take their employers to court to seek justice.
He took the matter to court to resolve the ongoing property dispute with his neighbor.

senses.definition: to share opportunities or responsibilities with others in a fair and orderly manner

examples.x: Couples should take turns doing household chores for a fair distribution of tasks.
Children often take turns playing with the toy to avoid conflicts.
In the meeting, team members took turns presenting their ideas.
During the game, participants take turns rolling the dice and making moves.
In a cooperative environment, employees take turns leading meetings to promote teamwork.

senses.definition: to firmly hold a position or opinion on an issue, often for moral, ethical, or social reasons

examples.x: The company took a stand against child labor.
I'm taking a stance against the new immigration policy.
The student took a stand against the school's dress code.
We will take a stance to support the community's well-being and progress.
She took a stand against animal cruelty by advocating for stricter laws.

senses.definition: to make a rough estimate or prediction, especially when one does not have complete or precise information

examples.x: Take a guess at how old I am.
I'm not sure what the answer is, but I'll take a guess.
She took a guess about the price of the antique, even though she wasn't certain.
Can you take a guess at what's in the box?

senses.definition: to look at something for a brief time

examples.x: I love having a gander at other people's homes - it gives me ideas.
I was surprised to hear about such expensive watches and took a gander on their website.
I heard there's something interesting happening down the street; let's take a gander and see what's going on.
Before we buy the book, let's take a gander at the first few pages to see if it's engaging.

senses.definition: used to show that one does not care if one's offer is accepted or rejected one will not negotiate further

examples.x: That's the deal—take it or leave it
I can sell you the car for $10,000, take it or leave it.
Our final offer is $500 for the antique table, take it or leave it.
The apartment lease is for one year at $1,200 per month, take it or leave it.

senses.definition: to experience a sudden setback, failure, or decline

examples.x: After a strong start, the company took a tumble in the stock market due to unexpected losses.
She was at the top of her class, but her grades took a tumble when she started missing classes.
The team had a promising season until their star player took a tumble and suffered a season-ending injury.
The politician's popularity took a tumble after a series of scandals were revealed.
The project was going well until a critical mistake caused it to take a tumble.

senses.definition: to act in a way that is overly familiar or inappropriate without permission or proper authority

examples.x: He took liberties with the company's expense account, spending money on personal items without authorization.
She felt uncomfortable when he took liberties with her personal space, invading her boundaries without permission.
He took liberties with the truth, exaggerating his accomplishments to impress others.
The director took liberties with the script, making changes that altered the meaning of the original story.
He took liberties with his power, using his position to exploit others for personal gain.

senses.definition: to not go to work or school for a certain period of time to take care of personal matters or recharge one's energy and focus

examples.x: She decided to take time off to recover from the flu.
He took time out to focus on his personal projects.
They encouraged employees to take time out for family events.
She plans to take time off during the holidays.
He often takes time out to reflect on his goals.

senses.definition: to borrow money from a bank or financial institution that must be paid back, usually with an extra amount added

examples.x: He decided to take out a loan to buy a new car.
They took out a loan to renovate their house.
They were approved to take out a loan for their small business.
He took out a loan to cover unexpected medical expenses.
She had to take out a loan to pay for her education.
